summary: Solving an absolute value equation or inequality means splitting it into two separate cases, one positive and one negative.
explanation: To solve \(|expression| = k\), set the expression equal to \(k\) AND to \(-k\), then solve each equation separately — always check both solutions in the original equation, since occasionally an extraneous root appears. For inequalities, \(|x| < k\) means \(x\) is within \(k\) of 0, giving a single connected range \(-k < x < k\); but \(|x| > k\) means \(x\) is farther than \(k\) from 0, giving two separate rays (\(x<-k\) or \(x>k\)) — remembering which pattern goes with which symbol ("less than" stays connected, "greater than" splits apart) is the key skill. If \(k<0\), an equation \(|expression|=k\) has no solution, since absolute value can't be negative.
